item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Excuse my language.

+ show block
– hide block

FUCKING. NIGHTMARE. FUEL. +1

It is possible that SCP-4068-1-B instances are at least partially aware of their condition.

Poor performance reviews result in the [DATA EXPUNGED] of employees.

SCP-4068-1 instances show no apparent signs of discomfort.

Moving the needle to my bleeding-edge pain points by the out-of-pocket disruptor drilling down from outside the box in a lovely little hotel in the Rockies.

Mind effecting SCP done fucking RIGHT. The corporate jab is alright but that's the foil for this horror show, it's what brings it down to earth relatably. If you let yourself believe that office suits have actually been co-opted by a memetic virus and are imprisoned in their own minds… just remember, some day you'll be old and wearing that suit too.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I like this one quite a bit. Nice work. +1

item.title: Re: +1
item.content:encoded: SCP-4985 remains my favorite lampoon of corporate culture. Also great take on the Cliche-Con's compulsive effect.

item.title: Re: Junior Staff Post
item.content:encoded:
Thanks for finding that. You're right, their terms seem very confusing. I replaced the image with a free stock one and added text. The new image is sourced from here, from Pexels, and the photographer is Eberhard Grossgasteiger:

https://www.pexels.com/photo/bird-s-eye-photography-of-mountain-1624496/

item.title: Re: Junior Staff Post
item.content:encoded: I've found Canva's official License agreement and A more summarized version of it, I haven't read through them entirely, but as far as a I can tell, the licensing for Canva is highly confusing. I think from what I read is, if the background is a stock image that you used directly from Canva, that's where problems/complications arise in the licensing.

item.title: Re: Junior Staff Post
item.content:encoded:
It's one I made myself using Canva:

https://www.canva.com/

and the image/design was free to use and provided by them. Is there anything else I need to specifically do to source it?

item.title: Junior Staff Post
item.content:encoded:
Currently, you have not sourced the image used in this article, and as such may be a violation of the SCP wiki's license (CC-BY-SA 3.0). You have 24 hours to source the image used. If you have not sourced the image within the allotted time frame, or the source is noncompliant with the SCP wiki's license, then we'll have to take the image down.

EDIT: The image has been sourced, and is cc-compliant. As such, it can remain on this article.
Good jab at the corporations. This and 3442 do a good job, although this leans to the fun side :)

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: It's good enough for me to upvote, but I think there are some areas, like the interview, that aren't quite satisfactory. For instance you refer to violence by -1 instances to regular employees, but never develop that. Maybe move the interview up higher or cut it, and have the list of -2 instances come last, with the last entry ending on a cliffhanger or interesting event.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I'm not especially a fan of the Conprocs here but I think the rest of the article more than redeems that. I echo Mortos' comment that the way this progresses into the -B's is really what set me over into a +1 vote.

For the conprocs, I think the idea of quarantining and interviewing and potentially gigantic office or corporate campus when this phrase comes up is really unreasonable. I'm not sure I have a better suggestion. SCPF kind of has to take on impossible tasks like that for the benefit of all, so I can get it even if I don't especially like it.

Good work!

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I like this one. It's a bit dense as mentioned in the comment above but the idea is cool, the table has some pretty good stuff in there and the idea that these -B instances are somewhat aware of what's happened to them is quite unsettling. +1 from me.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
This is missing a rating module.

I don't dislike this enough to downvote, but I will say this is dense and difficult to read, especially considering how straight-forward the anomaly is. I feel like with some tweaking this could be a -J. It's an anomaly that forces people to use corporate jargon, and it can be written with corporate jargon, like in the interview at the end. If it's going to be on the main list, it just needs more to it than "corporations be crazy".

Also, if this anomaly actually has the capability of affecting the top 500 corporations, then it is Keter. That is a lot of data to track and contain. Think of the amount of resources it would take to monitor ALL of the corporate communications at, say, Wal-Mart. Then add 499 more corporations of that size to that list. We're talking about an anomaly that affects the vast majority of the American work force plus a huge chunk of overseas workers too.

On a second reading, it's not that bad. Thanks for taking my feedback into account though. It makes me feel special.
